article.title: The Prompt as Expression: A Critical Analysis of the U.S. Copyright Office's Stance on AI Authorship
article.para: The U.S. Copyright Office (USCO), in its recent guidance, has drawn a firm line in the sand: works generated by artificial intelligence are not copyrightable if the human contribution is limited to providing a text prompt.1 The rationale rests on a cornerstone of copyright law—the idea-expression dichotomy—and a specific interpretation of creative control. The USCO argues that a prompt is merely an unprotectable "idea," and because the AI model's process is unpredictable and not fully controlled by the user, the human prompter cannot be considered the "author" of the final expressive output.3

This position, while attempting to preserve the "bedrock" requirement of human authorship, is founded on a flawed analogy, creates a significant logical paradox when contrasted with the debate around AI training data, and carries the profound, unintended consequence of disenfranchising creators who rely on assistive technologies for expression.
